findings.title: No authentication middleware defined for any backend API service
findings.description: Tasks 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 define public-facing API endpoints (catalog, RMS, finance, vetting, social media) but none of the subtasks implement authentication or authorization middleware. Decision point dp-6 identifies JWT vs mTLS as an open question but no task actually implements the chosen mechanism. All endpoints appear to be unauthenticated, meaning anyone with network access could create invoices, process payments, run vetting checks, approve social media posts, or modify rentals.
findings.recommendation: Add a dedicated subtask under each backend service (or as a shared library task) to implement JWT-based authentication middleware with role-based access control. Define roles (admin, operator, customer, service-account, morgan-agent) and enforce authorization on every endpoint. Create a shared auth library that all Rust/Axum and Go/gRPC services consume. This should be implemented before the API endpoint tasks, not deferred to production hardening.

findings.title: Stripe webhook endpoint lacks signature verification task validation
findings.description: Task 4004 mentions implementing Stripe webhook signature verification, which is good. However, the webhook endpoint (POST /v1/webhooks/stripe) is defined alongside the main API router with no mention of exempting it from rate limiting or ensuring it's excluded from CSRF protections that might interfere. More critically, there is no task to ensure the webhook endpoint is not authenticated with the same JWT mechanism as other endpoints (Stripe cannot send JWTs), creating a potential conflict when auth is added.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly document that the Stripe webhook endpoint must be excluded from JWT authentication middleware and instead rely solely on Stripe signature verification. Add a subtask to validate that the webhook secret is not logged or exposed in error responses. Ensure the webhook endpoint has its own dedicated rate limiting configuration separate from the general API.

findings.title: PII stored in multiple services without encryption at rest strategy
findings.description: Customer PII (names, phone numbers, email addresses, company information, credit data) is stored across multiple PostgreSQL schemas (rms, finance, vetting, crm) and potentially in Redis caches. There is no task addressing encryption at rest for PostgreSQL or Redis, no task for PII field-level encryption, and no data classification exercise. The vetting service (Task 5) stores particularly sensitive credit reports and business intelligence data.
findings.recommendation: Add a task to enable PostgreSQL encryption at rest via storage-level encryption (encrypted PVCs) or CNPG's built-in encryption support. For highly sensitive fields (credit scores, SSN-equivalent data, API keys stored in DB), implement application-level column encryption. Add a data classification document identifying PII fields across all schemas and their required protection levels. Configure Redis to use encrypted connections (TLS) and consider encrypted PVCs for Redis persistence.

findings.title: SQL injection risk in dynamic query construction for filtering/pagination
findings.description: Multiple services implement dynamic filtering and pagination with user-supplied parameters (category_id, search ILIKE, min_price, max_price, date_from, date_to, page, per_page). Tasks 2003, 3005, 4003, and 4006 build dynamic SQL queries. While sqlx (Rust) and pgx (Go) provide parameterized queries, the implementation details describe constructing filters that could lead to SQL injection if string interpolation is used instead of parameter binding, especially for the ILIKE search and dynamic ORDER BY clauses.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly require in each implementation task that ALL user-supplied values must use parameterized queries (bind parameters). For dynamic ORDER BY and column-name filters, use an allowlist pattern (map user input to a predefined set of valid column names) rather than interpolating user input. Add integration tests that attempt SQL injection payloads in search, filter, and sort parameters.

findings.title: Morgan agent MCP tools have no authorization boundary
findings.description: Tasks 7004 and 7005 register MCP tools that give the Morgan AI agent the ability to create invoices, process payments, approve customers, publish social media posts, modify rentals, and access credit reports. There is no authorization layer between Morgan and backend services — the agent can invoke any tool without restriction. Task 7007 mentions 'role-based access control to skills' for admin vs non-admin, but this is enforced via Signal phone number matching, which is trivially spoofable, and there's no mention of how web chat users are authenticated.
findings.recommendation: Implement a proper authorization layer for MCP tools: (1) Morgan's service account should have a dedicated JWT/API key with scoped permissions per backend service. (2) Distinguish between customer-initiated actions (which should require customer identity verification) and agent-autonomous actions. (3) Implement approval gates for high-risk actions (payment processing, customer vetting approval, social media publishing) rather than letting the AI execute them unilaterally. (4) Log all MCP tool invocations to an audit trail. (5) Implement rate limiting on sensitive tool invocations.

findings.title: Placeholder API keys in Kubernetes Secrets with no rotation strategy at creation time
findings.description: Task 1006 explicitly states 'Use placeholder values for now (to be replaced with real credentials before production).' This creates a significant risk that placeholder values will make it to production or that the process for replacing them is undocumented and error-prone. Additionally, secrets are stored as plain Kubernetes Secrets (base64 encoded, not encrypted), and there is no mention of using an external secrets manager.
findings.recommendation: Never commit placeholder API keys — even to dev clusters. Use a sealed-secrets or external-secrets-operator pattern from the start. Define a clear secret provisioning workflow: (1) Use External Secrets Operator with a vault backend (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or similar) from day one. (2) If that's not feasible immediately, at minimum enable Kubernetes etcd encryption for Secrets at rest. (3) Add a pre-deployment check that validates no placeholder values exist in production secrets. (4) The rotation tasks in Task 10 are good but come too late — secret hygiene should be established in Task 1.

findings.title: File upload in Social Media Engine lacks comprehensive security validation
findings.description: Task 6003 implements image upload with basic type validation (JPEG, PNG, WebP) and size limits, but does not mention: scanning uploaded files for malware/embedded scripts, stripping EXIF metadata that may contain GPS coordinates or device info, validating file magic bytes (not just extension/MIME), or preventing path traversal in generated S3 keys.
findings.recommendation: Add to the upload task: (1) Validate file magic bytes match declared MIME type. (2) Strip EXIF metadata from uploaded images before storage (using sharp's metadata removal). (3) Generate S3 keys using UUIDs only — never incorporate user-supplied filenames. (4) Set Content-Disposition: attachment on S3 objects to prevent inline execution. (5) Consider adding ClamAV scanning for uploaded files, or at minimum validate image dimensions/integrity by actually parsing the image.

findings.title: Signal-CLI pod stores registration state with insufficient access controls
findings.description: Task 1005 deploys Signal-CLI with a PersistentVolumeClaim storing sensitive registration data (private keys, session data). The Signal registration state represents a highly sensitive credential — if compromised, an attacker could impersonate Morgan on Signal. There is no mention of encrypting the PVC, restricting pod-level access, or backup procedures for the registration data.
findings.recommendation: Encrypt the Signal-CLI PVC at the storage class level. Restrict access to the Signal-CLI pod via NetworkPolicy (only Morgan agent should communicate with it). Add a backup strategy for the registration data. Consider running Signal-CLI with a read-only root filesystem and minimal Linux capabilities. Implement monitoring/alerting for unexpected access to the Signal-CLI service.

findings.title: No dependency auditing or pinning strategy across services
findings.description: Across all service initialization tasks (2001, 3001, 4001, 5001, 6001), dependencies are listed by name and major version but there is no mention of: lockfile enforcement (Cargo.lock, go.sum, bun.lockb), dependency vulnerability scanning (cargo audit, govulncheck, npm audit), pinning container base images to digests, or scanning container images for CVEs.
findings.recommendation: Add a cross-cutting task (or subtask in each service) for: (1) Commit lockfiles (Cargo.lock, go.sum, bun.lockb) to version control. (2) Pin Dockerfile base images to SHA256 digests, not just tags. (3) Add cargo-audit / govulncheck / npm audit to CI pipelines. (4) Add Trivy or Grype container image scanning to CI. (5) Use Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency update PRs. This should be part of each service's scaffold task, not deferred.

findings.title: Web chat WebSocket endpoint has no authentication or rate limiting
findings.description: Task 7008 implements a WebSocket endpoint (/ws/chat) for the Morgan web chat widget with CORS headers and session management, but no authentication mechanism. Any client can connect and consume Morgan's AI processing resources. There is no rate limiting on WebSocket messages, no protection against abuse (spam, prompt injection), and no client identity verification.
findings.recommendation: Implement: (1) A lightweight authentication mechanism for the chat widget (e.g., short-lived tokens generated by the frontend's server-side, or CAPTCHA verification before chat initiation). (2) Per-session message rate limiting (e.g., max 10 messages/minute). (3) Connection limits per IP address. (4) Input sanitization and length limits on chat messages. (5) Monitor for abuse patterns and implement automatic session termination for abusive behavior.

findings.title: Database connections not explicitly configured for TLS
findings.description: Tasks 1002 (PostgreSQL) and 1003 (Redis) deploy databases but do not mention enabling TLS for client connections. Backend service connection strings (tasks 2001, 3001, 4001, 5001, 6001) read from ConfigMap but do not specify sslmode=require or TLS client configuration. Data in transit between application pods and database pods is unencrypted.
findings.recommendation: Configure CloudNative-PG to enable TLS on the PostgreSQL server (CNPG supports this natively via the Cluster CR's `certificates` section). Set connection strings to include `sslmode=require` or `sslmode=verify-full`. For Redis/Valkey, enable TLS using the operator's TLS configuration. Update all service connection configurations to use TLS-enabled endpoints.

findings.title: Application-level audit trails are incomplete
findings.description: Task 10004 enables Kubernetes API server audit logging, but application-level audit trails are sparse. Only the RMS inventory service (task 3007) creates audit log records for check-in/check-out. There is no application-level audit logging for: finance operations (invoice creation, payment processing, refunds), vetting decisions, social media approvals/publications, or Morgan agent actions. These are critical for compliance (SOX for finance, GDPR for customer data access).
findings.recommendation: Add an audit logging middleware/service that records: (1) All finance mutations (invoice CRUD, payments, refunds) with actor identity, timestamp, and before/after state. (2) All vetting pipeline executions and score decisions. (3) All social media approval/rejection/publish actions with approver identity. (4) All Morgan agent MCP tool invocations with input parameters and results. Store audit records in a dedicated audit schema in PostgreSQL with append-only access controls. Ship audit logs to Loki alongside infrastructure audit logs.

findings.title: No pod security standards or security contexts defined
findings.description: None of the Kubernetes deployment tasks (1005, 7001, 9003) mention configuring pod security contexts (runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, drop ALL capabilities, no privilege escalation). Without these, containers run with broader privileges than necessary, increasing the blast radius of a container escape.
findings.recommendation: Add pod security contexts to all Deployment manifests: (1) runAsNonRoot: true, (2) readOnlyRootFilesystem: true (with emptyDir tmpfs for writable paths), (3) allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, (4) capabilities: drop: [ALL]. Apply the 'restricted' Pod Security Standard at the namespace level using PodSecurity admission controller labels. This should be part of the initial namespace setup in task 1001.

findings.title: No CORS configuration for backend API services
findings.description: The backend API services (Equipment Catalog, Finance, Vetting) do not mention CORS configuration. The website frontend will make cross-origin requests to these APIs. Without proper CORS headers, either the APIs will reject legitimate frontend requests, or overly permissive CORS will be added as a quick fix, exposing APIs to cross-origin attacks from malicious sites.
findings.recommendation: Add CORS middleware configuration to each backend service with: (1) Allowlisted origins (only the production domain and local dev). (2) Explicit allowed methods and headers. (3) Credentials support only where needed. (4) Short preflight cache max-age. Use tower-http's CorsLayer for Rust/Axum services.

findings.title: ConfigMap stores sensitive reference information in plain text
findings.description: Task 1007 creates a ConfigMap (sigma1-infra-endpoints) containing connection strings and secret references. While it uses _SECRET_REF pointers rather than actual credentials, the ConfigMap itself contains service hostnames, ports, and bucket names that reveal internal architecture. ConfigMaps are not encrypted in etcd by default and are readable by any pod with the appropriate RBAC.
findings.recommendation: Verify that the ConfigMap truly contains only non-sensitive configuration (hostnames, ports, bucket names) and that all actual credentials are in Secrets. Enable etcd encryption at rest for both Secrets and ConfigMaps. Consider using a service mesh or DNS-based service discovery instead of a centralized ConfigMap to reduce the information density in a single resource.

findings.title: AI prompt injection risk in Morgan agent and Social Media Engine
findings.description: The Morgan agent (Task 7) accepts free-text input from customers via Signal, phone, and web chat, passing it to an LLM that can invoke MCP tools with real business consequences. The Social Media Engine (Task 6004) passes user context to AI for caption generation. There is no mention of prompt injection defenses, input sanitization before LLM processing, or output validation after LLM tool invocation.
findings.recommendation: Implement: (1) Input sanitization layer before LLM processing that strips known prompt injection patterns. (2) Output validation on LLM tool invocation parameters — the LLM's requested tool parameters should be validated against schemas before execution. (3) Confirmation gates for destructive or high-value actions (Morgan should confirm with the human before processing payments or approving vetting). (4) Rate limiting on tool invocations per conversation session. (5) Monitor and log LLM interactions for anomaly detection.

findings.title: No container image registry trust policy
findings.description: Tasks reference specific container images (signal-cli-rest-api, Redis alpine, etc.) but there is no policy for trusted registries, image signing, or admission control to prevent deploying untrusted images.
findings.recommendation: Define a trusted registry policy (e.g., only allow images from specific registries). Consider implementing image signing with cosign/sigstore and admission control via Kyverno or OPA Gatekeeper to enforce signed images. At minimum, always use specific version tags or SHA digests rather than 'latest' tags.

strengths: Task 9006 implements default-deny NetworkPolicies with explicit allow rules, following the principle of least privilege for network access.
Task 10 provides a comprehensive production hardening phase covering RBAC, secret rotation, and audit logging with proper Grafana/Loki integration.
The plan uses separate Kubernetes namespaces for different concern areas (databases, sigma1, openclaw), providing basic isolation boundaries.
Stripe webhook signature verification is explicitly called out in task 4004, preventing forged payment events.
The use of Effect.Schema in the Social Media Engine (Task 6) provides runtime type validation for all request/response data, reducing type-related vulnerabilities.
Redis AUTH is configured from the start (task 1003), preventing unauthenticated cache access.
Task 9007 includes comprehensive failover testing with documented recovery procedures, which is essential for operational security.
The plan separates infrastructure secrets into individual Kubernetes Secrets per service rather than a monolithic secret, limiting blast radius of a single secret compromise.
summary: The plan has a critical gap: no backend service implements authentication or authorization middleware despite handling sensitive financial, customer, and operational data. Multiple services expose unauthenticated endpoints for payments, vetting, and rentals. PII protection is inadequate with no encryption at rest or in transit (database TLS) defined in the initial setup. The production hardening tasks (9, 10) address some concerns but arrive too late — security fundamentals like auth, TLS, and audit logging should be established during initial service development, not retroactively bolted on. The Morgan AI agent's unrestricted MCP tool access combined with no prompt injection defenses presents a high-risk attack surface.
